title: Tenant Handback and Cost Stewardship Runbook
preview:
Purpose
======

This runbook standardizes the transition (handback) of an enterprise tenant from the onboarding project team to steady-state Customer Success (CS) and Platform Operations. Its primary goals are to: 
- Ensure operational ownership and alert routing are correctly assigned. 
- Lock down cost controls an...
text:
Purpose
======

This runbook standardizes the transition (handback) of an enterprise tenant from the onboarding project team to steady-state Customer Success (CS) and Platform Operations. Its primary goals are to: 
- Ensure operational ownership and alert routing are correctly assigned. 
- Lock down cost controls and validate billing telemetry. 
- Confirm SLO coverage and QBR data availability. 
- Provide clear escalation and rollback procedures for the first 90 days after handback.

Scope
-----

Applies to: Dedicated and Private tenants (reserved capacity and VPC/on-prem) that completed provisioning and initial canaries. Excludes short-term PoCs and internal developer sandboxes.

Key Definitions
---------------

- Handback: formal transfer of operational responsibility from Onboarding Squad -> Customer Success + Platform Ops.
- Cost Stewardship: ongoing controls and alerts to prevent...

title: Customer Model Economics Deep Dive Template
preview:

This template is a standardized, customer-facing deep-dive used during QBRs and technical review sessions to explain the economics of a customer's inference footprint on Redwood. It focuses on translating telemetry and billing signals into actionable recommendations: model mix, batching and cache strategy,...
text:
Summary:

This template is a standardized, customer-facing deep-dive used during QBRs and technical review sessions to explain the economics of a customer's inference footprint on Redwood. It focuses on translating telemetry and billing signals into actionable recommendations: model mix, batching and cache strategy, quantization/variant choices, and reserved capacity sizing.

Audience:
- Customer Success Manager (CSM)
- Solutions Engineer (SE)
- Customer SRE / Platform lead
- Finance contact for the customer (optional)

When to use:
- Quarterly business reviews (QBR) where cost/perf is a top objective
- Post-cutover stabilization if spend or latency deviates from expectations
- Prior to pricing or dedicated capacity negotiations

Prerequisites and data sources:
1) Time window: last 28 / 90 / 365 days (choose based on volatility).
2) Telemetry sources:
 - Redwood inference telemetry (p...

title: Tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling playbook
preview:
Summary

This playbook describes the operational procedures, validation tests, and escalation steps for tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling for Dedicated and Private tenants. It targets customer-success, SRE, and platform engineers responsible for onboarding enterprise customers with unpredictable burst...
text:
Summary

This playbook describes the operational procedures, validation tests, and escalation steps for tenant egress fallback and fair-share throttling for Dedicated and Private tenants. It targets customer-success, SRE, and platform engineers responsible for onboarding enterprise customers with unpredictable burst traffic or constrained egress capacity.

Goals

- Ensure predictability for tenant traffic when outbound (egress) capacity is constrained.
- Maintain latency SLOs for high-priority tenants while applying fair-share policies to noisy neighbors.
- Provide a deterministic automated fallback that preserves availability and preserves customer data guarantees.

When to use this playbook

Use this playbook during:
1) Pre-production validation for Dedicated/Private tenant cutovers.
2) Go-live where tenant traffic patterns are unknown or have burst risk.
3) Incidents where egress l...

title: Client-proxy Shaping & Adaptive Cost-cap Handoff Playbook
preview:
Overview
This playbook documents a proven integration pattern for enterprise customers that run client-side proxies, API gateways, or feature-specific SDKs and want to negotiate an explicit cost and quality handoff with Redwood's ingress fairshare system. The pattern reduces noisy-neighbor impact, enforces per-featu...
text:
Overview
This playbook documents a proven integration pattern for enterprise customers that run client-side proxies, API gateways, or feature-specific SDKs and want to negotiate an explicit cost and quality handoff with Redwood's ingress fairshare system. The pattern reduces noisy-neighbor impact, enforces per-feature cost caps before requests reach Redwood, and preserves billing fidelity for reconciliation.

Audience
- Customer Success Engineers (CSEs) and Solutions Engineers
- Customer Platform / SRE teams
- Finance and Cost Ops contacts involved in usage reconciliation

Objectives
- Enforce feature-level hard cost caps at the edge to prevent runaway spend.
- Maintain observable request provenance so Redwood billing attributes usage correctly.
- Reduce server-side throttles and fallbacks by 50% for shaped tenants.
- Provide graceful degradation semantics (priority lanes + adaptive b...

title: Automated Degradation Classifier and Customer Failover Runbook
preview:
Overview

This runbook describes the automated degradation classifier (ADC) used by Customer Success and SRE to detect service-quality regressions for enterprise tenants, validate safe failover eligibility, and orchestrate tiered customer notifications and remediation. It documents classification thresholds, telemet...
text:
Overview

This runbook describes the automated degradation classifier (ADC) used by Customer Success and SRE to detect service-quality regressions for enterprise tenants, validate safe failover eligibility, and orchestrate tiered customer notifications and remediation. It documents classification thresholds, telemetry mappings, alert routing, escalation windows, and step-by-step procedures for automated and manual remediation.

Goals

- Detect real customer-impacting degradation (latency, error-rate, model-quality drift) with <80% false-positive rate in steady-state traffic.
- Automate non-destructive mitigations (routing to fallback model variants, traffic shaping) within 5 minutes of classification.
- Provide clear, auditable customer communications and playbook for escalation to engineering/ops.

Scope

- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants and high-volume Hosted API customers...

title: Enterprise LLM Behavior Delta Detection and Customer Triage Playbook
preview:
Summary:
This playbook documents a repeatable, measurable process for detecting and triaging behavioral deltas (quality regressions or shifts) in LLM outputs that materially affect enterprise customers. It focuses on automated detection signals, deterministic first-line triage, evidence packaging for root-cause anal...
text:
Summary:
This playbook documents a repeatable, measurable process for detecting and triaging behavioral deltas (quality regressions or shifts) in LLM outputs that materially affect enterprise customers. It focuses on automated detection signals, deterministic first-line triage, evidence packaging for root-cause analysis (RCA), and customer-safe communications. Use this playbook when telemetry or customers indicate unexpected changes in output behavior ( hallucinations, repeated refusal patterns, persona drift, or factual regressions ).

Scope:
- Applies to Redwood-hosted and Dedicated tenants (hosted API and dedicated capacity).
- Covers model serving changes, quantization/rewrites, prompt-layer changes, routing/fanout, and inference runtime rollouts.
- Does NOT replace safety escalation for clear safety violations; refer to the LLM Safety Escalation Matrix for safety-first incidents....

title: Ingress proxy connection entropy: intermittent 50x errors and mitigation runbook
preview:
Summary:
This document describes a recurring class of production incidents we term 'ingress proxy connection entropy' that manifest as intermittent 502/503/504 errors and short spikes of failed model fanout requests. It provides:

- clear symptom definitions and diagnostic queries
- an immediate mitigation runbook f...
text:
Summary:
This document describes a recurring class of production incidents we term 'ingress proxy connection entropy' that manifest as intermittent 502/503/504 errors and short spikes of failed model fanout requests. It provides:

- clear symptom definitions and diagnostic queries
- an immediate mitigation runbook for oncall and customer success engineers
- long‑term engineering fixes and rollout guidance
- test and verification checklists for customer-facing cutovers

Scope:
Applies to: Redwood-hosted ingress proxies and customer-managed sidecar proxies that perform request fanout to model serving pools (Hosted, Dedicated, Private). Not intended for client SDK bugs or pure model runtime failures (see separate model runtime runbooks).

Background and root-cause patterns observed:
We have observed several distinct contributing factors across incidents:

1) Ephemeral port churn + NAT ta...

title: Observability Privacy Filter and Keyed Telemetry Onboarding Playbook
preview:

text:
Purpose:
This playbook describes the recommended process for onboarding enterprise customers to Redwood's keyed telemetry pipeline with a privacy-filtering layer ("Observability Privacy Filter") between customer data sources and Redwood observability systems. It is intended to ensure compliance with customer privacy constraints, minimize PII leakage, and preserve the fidelity required for SLO/QBR reporting and cost accounting.

Scope:
- Applies to Dedicated and Private customers integrating their application telemetry (request traces, token-level cost attributions, custom metrics) into Redwood observability.
- Covers contract schema negotiation, filter configuration, implementation patterns, validation, rollout, and post-onboarding handoff to customer ops and Redwood CS.
- Does not replace legal data processing agreements; coordinate with Security & Legal for restricted data.

Audienc...

Summary:
This playbook defines steps, telemetry gate criteria, and cost-gating policies for cross-regional enterprise tenant cutovers. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, Solutions Engineers, SRE on-call, and the customer’s cloud/networking contact. title: Hosted API: Intermittent streaming disconnects (March 2026) - known issue + tracking
preview:
## Overview
Some customers have reported intermittent disconnections during streaming responses on the Hosted API (OpenAI-compatible streaming/SSE). The symptoms often present as truncated completions, missing final chunk/[DONE], or client-side errors such as "unexpected EOF" or "socket hang up".

This page document...
text:
## Overview
Some customers have reported intermittent disconnections during streaming responses on the Hosted API (OpenAI-compatible streaming/SSE). The symptoms often present as truncated completions, missing final chunk/[DONE], or client-side errors such as "unexpected EOF" or "socket hang up".

This page documents the known issue pattern observed in March 2026, affected environments, and where to track active customer cases.

## Customer-visible symptoms
- Streaming ends mid-generation (partial text returned)
- Client receives "connection closed" / "unexpected EOF"
- Missing final "done" signal
- Retries usually succeed

## Affected surfaces
- Hosted API OpenAI-compatible endpoints (chat.completions streaming=true)
- Regions observed: us-east, eu-west

## Workarounds (customer guidance)
1. Implement retries when a stream terminates unexpectedly (treat as retryable error).
2. Increa...

text:
## Purpose
This playbook standardizes how Customer Success (CS), Support, and Solutions Engineering (SE) engage SRE during **SLO / error budget** events. It covers:
- How to route reliability escalations (Hosted vs Dedicated)
- What data is required (to avoid slow back-and-forth)
- What we can and cannot commit to in customer-facing communications
- Expected response/updates by tier and severity

This is a **joint CS/SRE** process. It is designed to align with the tiered SLO program and error budget enforcement so that customer escalations map cleanly to what Engineering is measuring and acting on.

---

## Scope
**In scope**
- Production impact on Redwood API request paths: chat/completions, embeddings, streaming (SSE/WebSocket where applicable)
- Reliability symptoms: elevated 5xx, timeouts, increased latency (p95/p99), streaming disconnects
- Hosted (shared) and Dedicated (reserved...

## Purpose
This playbook defines how Customer Success (CS) and Support partner with Sales, Solutions Engineering (SE), and Security/GRC during late-stage security reviews. It standardizes:
- When and how to loop in Security/GRC
- How to handle urgent deadlines and incomplete questionnaires
- Escalation tiers, response SLAs, and handoff points

This is an internal operating document. It references restricted evidence processes (e.g., SOC 2 report) but does not include restricted artifacts.

## When to use this playbook
Use this playbook when any of the following occur:
- A prospect/customer requests SOC 2 report, pen test summary, subprocessor list, or other formal evidence.
- A prospect/customer sends a security questionnaire, RFP security section, or needs a security addendum.

text:
Overview
---------
This document is a pragmatic, step-by-step checklist for onboarding a new enterprise customer with proactive telemetry, SLO mapping, and QBR-ready artifacts. It is intended for Customer Success engineers, Solutions Engineers, and the customer engineering contact. The goal is to ensure that from day zero we have the signals, dashboards, and alerts required to operate the tenant within agreed SLAs and to produce reliable QBR metrics.

Scope
-----
- Applies to Hosted and Dedicated customers (Private deployments need network-specific gating described in the ‘Network & Security’ section).
- Covers: metric collection, trace and request tagging, SLO definition, alert thresholds, dashboarding, and QBR artifacts.
- Does not replace incident runbooks — use the incident-runbook specific to the service for live response.

Prerequisites (Customer / Redwood)
---------------------...

text:
## Purpose
This page standardizes **how Support starts and runs a Slack bridge** for customer-impacting escalations, and how/when we transition the conversation into **#incidents**.

Goals:
- Reduce time-to-triage by ensuring every bridge starts with complete context.
- Ensure Engineering/SRE can take action quickly without back-and-forth.
- Keep Jira (SUP tickets) and Slack synchronized so escalations are discoverable after the bridge ends.
- Ensure **one owner** is accountable for customer-facing updates (email / ticket / status page).

Non-goals:
- Replace the company incident process. If an issue meets incident criteria, we transition to #incidents and follow the incident playbook.

---

## Definitions
- **Support bridge:** A short-lived Slack channel created by Support to coordinate triage/mitigation for a customer escalation.
- **Incident channel:** A channel created/owned under...

## Purpose
This checklist standardizes what Support collects at intake so we can:
- Reduce time-to-triage and time-to-mitigation.
- Avoid avoidable back-and-forth (missing region/model/request IDs).
- Route faster to Known Issues vs. new investigations.
- Escalate with the correct context when paging SRE/Platform/Runtime.

Applies to: **Hosted API**, **Dedicated**, and **Private (VPC/on‑prem)**.

---

## 0) Before you respond: classify the inbound
**Identify these immediately (even if details are missing):**
- Customer tier: Self-serve / Dedicated / Private / Enterprise (contracted)
- Symptom type: Availability, latency, streaming, auth, quota/rate limits, console, billing/metering, audit logs, installer/upgrade
- Scope guess: Single user vs. single org vs. region-wide
- Time sensitivity: Is the customer blocked in production?

If it looks like a broad incident (multi-customer, multip...

## Purpose
This page defines the **required Jira fields, priority/severity rules, SLA timer usage, and linking/closure standards** for customer-facing Support tickets in the **SUP** project.

Goals:
- Reduce time-to-triage and time-to-mitigation with consistent, searchable ticket data.
- Ensure Support **SLAs** are tracked consistently (first response + update cadence) and align with Engineering on-call practices.
- Make escalations auditable: every page/bridge/incident should be linkable from the SUP ticket.

Scope:
- Applies to all SUP tickets (Hosted, Dedicated, Private).
- Applies to Support + CS when creating/updating SUP tickets.

Non-goals:
- Replacing incident process (#incidents + incident commander). Use SUP as the **customer-impact record** and handoff anchor.

---

## Golden rules (read first)
1) **One SUP ticket per customer issue** (even if Engineering work spans multipl...

## Purpose
This page is the single source of truth for **who Support/CS should page, when, and how** for customer-impacting issues across **Hosted**, **Dedicated**, and **Private** tiers.

Goals:
- Reduce time-to-triage and time-to-mitigation by routing issues to the correct on-call quickly.

## Purpose
This protocol defines **when and how Support/CS escalates to executive leadership** and what information must be included to enable fast, consistent decisions. It is designed to:
- Reduce time-to-decision for high-stakes customer-impacting events
- Ensure Support SLAs and Engineering incident processes stay aligned
- Standardize approvals for **credits**, **custom mitigations**, and **status page** actions

**This page is restricted** because executive escalations often contain customer-sensitive details (account context, contract terms, timelines, credit discussions).

## Scope
Use this protocol for:
- Customer-impacting incidents where leadership visibility or approval is required
- Enterprise/Dedicated/Private escalations with contractual/SLA implications
- Any request for credits, special handling, or deviations from standard process

Not in scope:
- Routine escalations...

title: Workaround: Streaming timeouts, disconnects, and reconnect guidance
preview:
## Summary
Some customers may see streamed responses disconnect mid-generation (connection reset, EOF), appear “truncated” (partial output), or hang until a client/proxy timeout occurs. This article describes common causes, recommended retry/backoff patterns, client timeout settings, and temporary mitigations (inclu...
text:
## Summary
Some customers may see streamed responses disconnect mid-generation (connection reset, EOF), appear “truncated” (partial output), or hang until a client/proxy timeout occurs. This article describes common causes, recommended retry/backoff patterns, client timeout settings, and temporary mitigations (including disabling HTTP/2 and adjusting keepalive).

**This is a workaround article.** It does not replace incident response. If you suspect a regional/platform degradation, follow escalation criteria below.

---

## Applies to
- **Products/Tiers:** Hosted API (most common), can also affect Dedicated (customer-side proxies) and Private (customer ingress settings)
- **Endpoints:** Streaming chat/completions (SSE / chunked streaming responses)
- **SDKs:** Python, TypeScript/Node, Go (and any custom HTTP client)
- **Regions:** Any (often reported as region-specific when tied to a ...

## Purpose
This page is the landing index for **Known Issues & Workarounds** used by **Support** and **Customer Success**. It is optimized for:
- Faster triage (copy/paste symptoms + quick checks)
- Lower repeat ticket volume (consistent workaround guidance)
- Clear escalation decisions (when to page SRE/Eng vs continue troubleshooting)

This index only includes issues that meet the **Known Issue publication criteria**: reproducible symptoms, validated workaround (or clear mitigation limits), and named owner + review cadence.

> Customer-sensitive details (account identifiers, internal hostnames, customer-specific configs) must not be added to KB pages or this index.

---

## How to use this index (Support/CS)
1) Identify the best matching issue by **symptom** and **tags** (tier/region/model).
2) Follow the linked **workaround article** and use the relevant **Support macro/snippet**.

Private deployments occasionally encounter upgrade failures where the **redwood-private-installer** reports progress but appears stuck at **"Running migrations"** (or repeatedly retries). This page describes:

This page defines Redwood Inference Support SLAs by customer tier (Self-serve Hosted API, Dedicated, Private, Enterprise). It standardizes:

text:
Overview:
This playbook defines the end-to-end escalation, containment, remediation, and post-incident review process for confirmed or strongly suspected exposures of customer-sensitive data (PII, proprietary customer content, model prompts with sensitive inputs, or retained telemetry). It applies to hosted Redwood API, Redwood Dedicated, and Redwood Private deployments. The document emphasizes fast containment, clear communication, legal and compliance coordination, and preserving evidence for forensic review.

Scope and definitions:
- Sensitive data: anything classified as PII, PCI, PHI, proprietary trade secrets, or customer-designated restricted data.
- Exposure: data confirmed to have been transmitted to an unauthorized destination (external cloud, public internet, third-party vendor without contract), or accessible to unauthorized internal actors.
- Suspected exposure: credible ...

title: Customer Comms Templates (Support + CS)
preview:
## Purpose
Use these templates for consistent, customer-safe communication during escalations across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers. These templates are designed for:
- Initial acknowledgement
- Mitigation / investigation updates

text:
## Purpose
Use these templates for consistent, customer-safe communication during escalations across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers. These templates are designed for:
- Initial acknowledgement
- Mitigation / investigation updates
- Workaround guidance (known issues)
- Resolution + follow-up (including next steps and optional RCA workflow)

These templates are written to reduce back-and-forth, align to Support SLAs, and avoid overpromising.

## Tone + content guidance (read before using)
**Be clear, calm, and specific.** Customers mostly want: confirmation we see the issue, what they should do now, what we’re doing, and when they’ll hear from us next.

### Do
- Acknowledge impact in the customer’s terms (errors, timeouts, latency, blocked workflow).
- Ask for the minimum viable diagnostics: request IDs, timestamps, region, model, SDK version, and whether streaming/non-streaming.
...

title: Technical Health Sentinel: Enterprise Post-Provision Monitoring & QBR Feed Playbook
preview:
Summary
=======
This playbook defines the Technical Health Sentinel (THS): a prescriptive set of telemetry monitors, smoke checks, and QBR signal extractions that Customer Success (CS) uses for enterprise post-provision monitoring (first 90 days + quarterly health reviews). THS is intended to catch operational, perf...
text:
Summary
=======
This playbook defines the Technical Health Sentinel (THS): a prescriptive set of telemetry monitors, smoke checks, and QBR signal extractions that Customer Success (CS) uses for enterprise post-provision monitoring (first 90 days + quarterly health reviews). THS is intended to catch operational, performance, and quality regressions before they impact the customer and to provide a repeatable set of artifacts and dashboards for QBRs.

Goals
-----
- Detect regressions in availability, latency, correctness, and cost within 1 business hour of occurrence.
- Provide a structured triage path to resolve common classes of customer-impacting issues within SLOs.
- Standardize QBR inputs so CS and Product can make data-driven decisions.

When to use this playbook
------------------------
Use THS for:
- All enterprise customers during the 90-day post-provision window.
- Any Dedicate...

title: Workaround: Audit log delivery latency (lag) — symptoms, expectations, and mitigations
preview:
## Summary
Some customers may observe audit log records arriving later than expected ("audit log lag"), typically during transient backlog events in the delivery pipeline. This article explains how to recognize the issue, what “expected” delivery looks like, which mitigations are safe to recommend, and how Support c...
text:
## Summary
Some customers may observe audit log records arriving later than expected ("audit log lag"), typically during transient backlog events in the delivery pipeline. This article explains how to recognize the issue, what “expected” delivery looks like, which mitigations are safe to recommend, and how Support confirms whether Redwood’s pipeline is backlogged.

**Use this page when:**
- Customer reports audit events are missing but later appear.
- Customer’s collector/export job is intermittently failing due to late-arriving records.
- Customer is validating compliance workflows and needs a clear expectation for delivery timing.

**Do not promise:** exact per-event delivery time guarantees beyond the published expectations below, or that “no events were dropped” before checking pipeline health and storage success metrics.

---

title: Workaround: 429 quota mismatch and rate‑limit headers
preview:
## Summary
Customers may receive HTTP **429 Too Many Requests** while their dashboard (or internal estimates) suggest they are under quota. In most cases, this is expected behavior caused by **short-window rate limits** (requests/minute, tokens/minute, concurrent requests) rather than **plan quota** (daily/monthly t...
text:
## Summary
Customers may receive HTTP **429 Too Many Requests** while their dashboard (or internal estimates) suggest they are under quota. In most cases, this is expected behavior caused by **short-window rate limits** (requests/minute, tokens/minute, concurrent requests) rather than **plan quota** (daily/monthly token or embedding allotment). In a smaller set of cases, the confusion is due to:
- Misinterpreting response headers (rate limit vs quota signals)
- SDK retry behavior causing unintentional traffic amplification
- Header inconsistencies that were addressed in an engineering fix (see “Status / ongoing fix”)

This article provides customer-safe troubleshooting steps, common misconfigurations, and Support validation steps.

---

## What a 429 means at Redwood
A **429** indicates the request was blocked by an enforcement policy. The most common categories are:
1) **Rate limit e...

text:
## Purpose
This page standardizes how Support assigns **Support Priority (P0–P4)** and how that maps to Engineering/SRE **Incident Severity (Sev0–Sev3)**. The goal is consistent language across:
- Jira SUP tickets (Support triage + SLA timers)
- Slack bridges / #incidents handoff
- Linear incident/follow-up workstreams
- Customer communications and escalation expectations

This rubric is designed to:
- Reduce time-to-triage by giving concrete triggers
- Prevent “severity inflation” while still paging quickly on real customer impact
- Align Support escalation thresholds with Engineering SLO alerting and on-call practices

---

## Definitions (what we mean by “Priority” vs “Severity”)
- **Support Priority (P0–P4):** How urgently Support must respond/coordinate based on **customer impact and contractual expectations**, including single-customer criticality.
- **Incident Severity (Sev0–Se...

title: Known issue: Streaming responses may freeze mid-generation (SSE stalls)
preview:
## Overview
Some customers have reported that streaming responses stop emitting tokens mid-generation while the HTTP connection remains open. The client may not receive an error or a terminal [DONE] event. This is typically described as a *freeze* or *stall* in server-sent events (SSE).

## Symptoms
- Streaming comp...
text:
## Overview
Some customers have reported that streaming responses stop emitting tokens mid-generation while the HTTP connection remains open. The client may not receive an error or a terminal [DONE] event. This is typically described as a *freeze* or *stall* in server-sent events (SSE).

## Symptoms
- Streaming completion stops mid-sentence.
- TCP/TLS connection remains open.
- No final event (e.g., [DONE]) is observed by the client.
- Client UI shows an infinite spinner or hangs waiting for more tokens.

## Where weve seen it
- Dedicated and Hosted API traffic, primarily under higher concurrency or with intermediaries (proxies/LBs).
- Internal reproduction was easiest when response compression (gzip) was applied to text/event-stream.

## Primary contributing factors (current understanding)
1. Buffering/backpressure in intermediaries when payload chunks are small (token-by-token).
2. ...

Dedicated GPU pools can occasionally enter an **autoscaling oscillation** pattern (rapid scale up/down or repeated rescheduling) that drives **latency spikes**, intermittent **5xx/timeout errors**, and unstable throughput. This article describes how Support/CS can identify the pattern quickly, apply safe ...
text:
## Summary
Dedicated GPU pools can occasionally enter an **autoscaling oscillation** pattern (rapid scale up/down or repeated rescheduling) that drives **latency spikes**, intermittent **5xx/timeout errors**, and unstable throughput. This article describes how Support/CS can identify the pattern quickly, apply safe short-term mitigations, and determine when to involve SRE/capacity.

This is a *workaround / operational guidance* article. Engineering follow-up for long-term guardrails is tracked in **ENG-4829**.

---

## Applies to
- **Product/Tier:** Redwood Dedicated
- **Common triggers:** sudden traffic shape change (burst or step-down), spiky token/sec, large variation in prompt length, or a new workload with higher concurrency.
- **Not typically applicable:** Hosted API (different fleet autoscaling), Private (customer-managed cluster autoscaling; similar symptoms but different knob...

## Purpose
This playbook explains how Customer Success (CS) and Support should use the **Incident Taxonomy (v1)** in customer communications.

Goals:
- Communicate clearly and consistently without speculating on root cause.
- Set correct expectations for mitigations and next updates.
- Trigger the right escalation path based on incident type, severity, and customer tier (Hosted / Dedicated / Private).
- Ensure comms align with remediation SLA expectations (time-to-mitigate/time-to-fix) and contractual commitments.

## Audience
- Support engineers and CS managers handling active incidents and escalations
- Incident Comms Owners (ICOs) for Dedicated/Private accounts
- Support leadership coordinating status-page updates and executive escalations

## Ground rules (what we say / don’t say)
### Use the taxonomy as *classification*, not a root-cause claim

text:
Summary:
This document defines the tenant-level contract between Redwood and enterprise customers for automated failover, observability telemetry, and cost-safeguard behavior. It is intended for customer success, platform engineers, SREs, and partner integration teams.

Goals:
- Keep customer-facing inference traffic available during service/resource degradation with predictable, auditable behavior.
- Ensure minimal false-positive escalation by defining telemetry thresholds and synthetic checks.
- Provide clear economic safeguards to prevent runaway billing while preserving critical availability.

Scope:
- Applies to Dedicated and Private tenants only (hosted tier has separate defaults).
- Covers ingress failover, model fallback, burst throttling, billing-protection limits, and telemetry signals used for QBRs.
- Excludes cross-region DR for air-gapped on-prem installs unless explicitl...

text:
## Overview
This sprint is a focused Customer Success & Support initiative to reduce repeat tickets and speed customer mitigation by publishing validated, copy/paste-ready workaround articles for our most common, highest-impact issues across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private tiers.

This plan defines:
- Selection criteria for sprint issues (what qualifies and what does not).
- Owners/reviewers and the publication workflow.
- A publication checklist to ensure articles are safe, accurate, and consistent with Support escalation workflows.
- Target metrics and how we will measure impact.

**Sprint window:** 2 weeks (Planning week + Execution week) with a follow-up week for adoption/cleanup.

---

## Goals
1. **Reduce time-to-triage** for high-frequency issues by making troubleshooting steps and required diagnostics consistent.
2. **Reduce time-to-mitigation** for customer-impacting incidents...

## Purpose
This page is a Support/CS reference for explaining **429** responses after the **Hot-route capacity protection** rollout. It covers:
- What customers may see (payload + headers)
- How to distinguish **quota** vs **rate-limit** vs **overload shedding/admission control**
- Recommended customer-side retry/backoff behavior
- When and how to escalate (and what data to collect)

This doc is written for Support and TAMs. For implementation details, see the Platform design/ADR pages linked in Related pages.

---

## What changed (customer-visible)
Beginning with the enterprise priority rollout, Redwood enforces **tier-aware capacity protection** on a set of “protected routes” (commonly: `/v1/chat/completions`, `/v1/embeddings`, `/v1/rerank`).

Customers may observe:
- **More predictable 429s** for best-effort/self-serve traffic during regional pressure.

text:
# Purpose
This SOP describes how Customer Success (CS) and Support intake, scope, and coordinate **customer-managed audit log export** requests across Hosted, Dedicated, and Private deployments.

# When to use
- A customer asks to export Redwood audit logs to their cloud storage or SIEM pipeline.
- A security questionnaire requires audit log delivery details and customer wants automated export.

# Request intake checklist (CS/Support)
Collect the following in the internal-support Jira ticket:
- Customer company
- Deployment mode: Hosted | Dedicated | Private
- Regions in use
- Desired sink: S3 | GCS | Azure Blob
- Destination details:
 - bucket/container name
 - prefix
 - destination region
- Identity method:
 - AWS assume-role / IRSA
 - GCP workload identity
 - Azure workload identity
- Retention intent (customer-owned)
- Operational contact + escalation contact
- Target date (if tie...

title: Activation SLA Exception & Timebox Playbook
preview:
Overview

It provides timeboxed steps for containment, investigation, mitigation, escalation, and customer communications to minimize time-to-stability while protecting cost and...
text:
Overview

Purpose:
This playbook defines a deterministic process for handling activation-window SLA exceptions during enterprise customer onboarding. It provides timeboxed steps for containment, investigation, mitigation, escalation, and customer communications to minimize time-to-stability while protecting cost and product quality.

When to use this playbook:
- Customer experiences degraded latency, elevated error rates, or cost-velocity exceeding onboarding projections within the first 30 days of activation.
- Automated health checks or customer-reported incidents indicate sustained SLA breach during activation that may impact go-live commitments.
- Normal runbooks (standard incident or SRE playbooks) have been attempted and the issue persists beyond the defined timebox thresholds.

Goals:
- Contain impact within defined timeboxes (Investigation: 60m, Mitigation candidate deploy: 12...

title: DNS and Certificate Onboarding Runbook — Private & Dedicated Tenants
preview:

This runbook documents the DNS and TLS/certificate acceptance, validation, and remediation steps required for enterprise customers during cutover to a Dedicated or Private deployment. It focuses on tenant-provided DNS records and customer-managed TLS certificates, and includes automated checks, manual verif...
text:
Summary:
This runbook documents the DNS and TLS/certificate acceptance, validation, and remediation steps required for enterprise customers during cutover to a Dedicated or Private deployment. It focuses on tenant-provided DNS records and customer-managed TLS certificates, and includes automated checks, manual verification commands, acceptance criteria, rollback options, and escalation contacts.

Scope and audience:
- Scope: Tenant DNS records (A/CNAME/ALIAS), DNS delegation patterns, split-horizon considerations, TLS certificate ingestion and rotation, ACME vs uploaded certs, OCSP stapling, and monitoring configuration for Dedicated and Private deployments.
- Audience: Customer Success engineers (CSE), Solutions Engineers (SE), SRE owners, and customer network/security teams.

Preconditions (what customer must provide before cutover):
1) FQDN(s) to be used for production (one or more...

## Purpose
This playbook is used by Support and Customer Success when a customer reports that the public status page does not reflect the impact they are experiencing ("status page says everything is OK, but we are failing"). It provides:
- A standard intake checklist to quickly validate what the customer is seeing.
- A clear escalation path to SRE/on-call and the incident Comms Lead.
- Approved customer-facing responses and guardrails.
- A consistent way to correct status page inaccuracies without increasing on-call burden.

This is a customer trust workflow. Our goals are (1) fast acknowledgement, (2) accurate classification to the right public component(s), and (3) avoiding speculative or customer-sensitive disclosures.

## When to use this playbook
Use this playbook when any of the following are true:
- Customer claims status page indicates "Operational" but they have errors/laten...

title: Streaming chat responses can hang or stall (SSE): symptoms, causes, and workarounds
preview:
h1. Overview
Some customers report that *streaming chat completions* (SSE, stream=true) appear to *hang* or *stall* mid-response. The connection may stay open but no new chunks arrive, and the client may never receive the terminal *[DONE]* marker.

This page consolidates known symptoms, likely causes, and recommende...
text:
h1. Overview
Some customers report that *streaming chat completions* (SSE, stream=true) appear to *hang* or *stall* mid-response. The connection may stay open but no new chunks arrive, and the client may never receive the terminal *[DONE]* marker.

This page consolidates known symptoms, likely causes, and recommended mitigations for Support/CS.

h1. Customer-visible symptoms
* Stream starts and then stops emitting chunks (customer wording: "hang", "freeze", "stuck").
* No explicit error returned by the API; request appears in-flight indefinitely.
* Some clients retry and create duplicate assistant messages.

Common alternate descriptions seen in tickets:
* "SSE freezes"
* "stream stops after first chunk"
* "never sends [DONE]"
* "EventSource waits forever"

h1. Likely causes (multiple can apply)
h2. 1) Network/proxy idle timeouts when the stream goes quiet
If the backend pauses token ...

text:
## Purpose
This page helps Support/CS respond to customer reports of new “overload protection” errors (circuit breakers + load shedding) on Redwood Hosted API.

Goals:
- Explain what the customer is seeing (stable semantics vs ambiguous timeouts/5xx).
- Provide canonical retry/backoff guidance to prevent retry storms.
- Show how to interpret correlation IDs so Engineering can quickly trace requests.
- Define escalation criteria and the minimum information to collect.

## What changed (customer-facing)
When the platform is saturated (GPU capacity, scheduler backlog, downstream dependency brownout), Redwood may reject requests *early* and predictably rather than allowing them to time out or generate random 5xx.

Customers may now see:
- **429** responses for admission / quota / per-tenant caps.
- **503** responses when a regional/model circuit breaker is open or the system is actively s...

title: Predictive Autoscaling (Dedicated) — Known Issues & Recommended Workarounds
preview:
h1. Overview
Predictive autoscaling is an opt-in mode for *Redwood Dedicated* that biases scale decisions using forecasted demand (time-of-day patterns, scheduled events, historical request rates/latency). Predictive mode *does not* override customer-configured *min/max bounds* and will fall back to reactive signals...
text:
h1. Overview
Predictive autoscaling is an opt-in mode for *Redwood Dedicated* that biases scale decisions using forecasted demand (time-of-day patterns, scheduled events, historical request rates/latency). Predictive mode *does not* override customer-configured *min/max bounds* and will fall back to reactive signals when forecasts are stale/unavailable.

This page lists the most common *known issues* observed during pilot/canary, how to recognize them, and recommended *workarounds* for Customer Success and Support. This is not a full runbook; for deep debugging and mitigations, use the dedicated on-call runbook linked above.

h2. Scope / When this page applies
* Applies to:* Dedicated deployments with predictive mode enabled (feature-flagged per account/deployment).
* Not applicable to:* Hosted API autoscaling or Dedicated deployments running reactive-only scaling.

h2. Quick definiti...

## Purpose
This playbook helps Customer Success (CS) and Support respond to Dedicated customer escalations involving autoscaling **min/max bounds** and optional **predictive mode**. It provides:
- Intake questions to quickly identify root cause
- How to interpret bounded-scaling metrics and dashboards
- Common misconfigurations and how to correct them safely
- Guidance for recommending min/max settings aligned with SLA and cost controls
- Escalation criteria and internal routing (SRE vs Infra vs Platform)

## Scope
Applies to **Redwood Dedicated** deployments using the scaling policy feature:
- Hard bounds: `min_replicas` and `max_replicas` (and/or GPU quota equivalents)
- Optional predictive mode (forecast-biased scaling) with reactive override

Out of scope: Hosted API scaling behavior, cross-region capacity placement, contract renegotiation.

## Purpose

 - Log sink target (Loki / Splunk HEC / Elasticsearch(OpenSearch) / OTLP endpoint).

title: Known Issue: Timeouts with Structured Output (JSON Schema) on Hosted API
preview:
## Summary
Some customers have observed intermittent timeouts when using the Hosted API structured output feature with JSON Schema constraints. The same prompts may succeed when structured output is disabled or when using tool-calling output.

This is typically caused by long-tail latency in the constrained decoding...
text:
## Summary
Some customers have observed intermittent timeouts when using the Hosted API structured output feature with JSON Schema constraints. The same prompts may succeed when structured output is disabled or when using tool-calling output.

This is typically caused by long-tail latency in the constrained decoding path, especially when schemas are large or contain combinators (e.g., anyOf/oneOf) and deeply nested arrays.

## Affected surface
- Product: Hosted API
- Endpoint: /v1/chat/completions
- Feature: structured output (JSON Schema)
- Regions: primarily us-east (reports), potential in other regions depending on workload

## Known impacted customers
The following customer reports have been validated by Support/Engineering:
- Kestrel Labs (enterprise) SUP-4812
- Northwind Analytics (POC) reported via CS email; correlated request IDs req_1f3b8c0c, req_6aa2d19e, req_09a3f7d1

## Sy...

title: PLG Signal Handoff: Sales ↔ Customer Success (CS) Process
preview:

This page defines how Redwood routes and follows up on PLG-to-sales signals without creating conflicting outreach or a poor customer experience. It clarifies:
- When Customer Success (CS) is notified vs. when Sales owns first outreach
- How to avoid duplicate touches (Sales vs. CS vs. Marketing) and manag...
text:
## Purpose
This page defines how Redwood routes and follows up on PLG-to-sales signals without creating conflicting outreach or a poor customer experience. It clarifies:
- When Customer Success (CS) is notified vs. when Sales owns first outreach
- How to avoid duplicate touches (Sales vs. CS vs. Marketing) and manage suppression windows
- How to use PLG signal history in QBRs and expansion planning

This process applies to the NA pilot and is the default operating model unless overridden by a named account plan.

## Background / What is a “PLG Signal”?
A PLG Signal is a scored, deduped account-level event created from product telemetry and intent behavior (e.g., sustained spend velocity, repeated 429s, Dedicated/Private docs interest, security FAQ views, multi-workspace expansion). Qualified signals are routed into HubSpot as:
- Company properties (latest signal type, score, timestamp...

Overview

Purpose: This playbook defines the activation milestones, customer health scoring model, routine health-check procedures, and handoff steps for Enterprise customers during the first 180 days (Day 0 -> Day 180). It is intended for CSMs, Solutions Engineering (SE), Support, and Onboarding Eng teams.

Scope: Applies to Dedicated and Private deployment customers and to any Hosted customers with enterprise contracts where the CSM is assigned.

Why this matters
- Early detection of friction reduces time-to-value and lowers incident risk.
- Consistent activation milestones improve predictability for Renewals and Expansion.
- A single, measurable health model aligns cross-functional teams on priorities.

## Purpose
This playbook defines the end-to-end process for handling Dedicated **Throughput SLO** misses or suspected misses. It is designed for Customer Success (CS) and Support to:
- Triage quickly and consistently.
- Engage the correct on-call teams (SRE / Infra / Observability) at the right time.
- Communicate clearly with customers, including status updates and written summaries.
- Route service-credit / compensation requests through the correct approval path.

This page is **restricted** because it includes internal paging triggers and credit approval guidance.

---

## What is the Dedicated Throughput SLO (customer-facing summary)
Dedicated Throughput SLO guarantees a **minimum sustained throughput floor** for an eligible Dedicated deployment under defined conditions.

**High-level definition (internal summary):**
- Metric: throughput measured as **billable output tokens per se...

text:
## Purpose
This page is an internal Customer Success / Solutions Engineering guide for configuring **Splunk HTTP Event Collector (HEC)** as a destination for the **SIEM-ready audit export (beta)** feature in Redwood Inference. It covers:

- What to confirm with the customer before enablement
- How to configure Splunk HEC and Redwood’s destination settings
- How to validate events end-to-end (including dedup expectations)
- Troubleshooting common Splunk-side and Redwood-side errors
- Setting correct beta expectations (delivery semantics, backfill, limitations)

This doc is written for Hosted + Dedicated beta customers. Private/on-prem deployments are **out of scope for beta**.

---

## TL;DR (for fast enablement)
1) Confirm customer has Splunk HEC enabled and can provide: **HEC endpoint URL**, **HEC token**, **index**, and preferred **sourcetype/source**.
2) Ensure the customer’s netwo...
